title: Career & Calling
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Work and Calling
summary: Scripture treats work as part of the creation design, not a consequence of the fall — and expands 'calling' far beyond vocational ministry to include every form of faithful labor.
body:
Work existed before sin entered the world. God placed Adam in the garden to work it and keep it [verse:gen.2.15]. Labor is part of the original design — a form of imaging God, who himself works in creation.

Paul elevated ordinary work with a single instruction: whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men [verse:col.3.23]. This does not spiritualize secular work — it reframes all work as service rendered to God, whether the task is preaching or plumbing.

The wisdom of Ecclesiastes is characteristically direct: there is nothing better than that a person should enjoy their work, for that is their lot [verse:eccl.3.22]. Satisfaction in daily labor is presented as a genuine gift from God, not a consolation prize for those who missed a 'higher calling.'

Bezalel is a striking example. God filled him with his Spirit specifically for craftsmanship — artistic skill, design, and metalwork [verse:exod.35.31-33]. The Spirit's empowerment was not limited to prophetic speech; it equipped hands.

Jesus spent the majority of his life as a craftsman in Nazareth before beginning public ministry. The silent years were not wasted — they were faithful work.

Scripture's vision of calling is wide enough to include every honest occupation done with integrity and offered to God.

verses.annotation: Work predates the fall — it is part of the creation design, not a punishment.
All work done heartily, as for the Lord — secular/sacred divide dissolved.
Enjoyment in work is a gift — not a lesser calling.
Bezalel filled with the Spirit for craftsmanship — the Spirit empowers hands, not just mouths.
Skilled workers stand before kings — excellence in craft has dignity.
Whether you eat or drink, do all to the glory of God — everything counts.

scholars.quote: Sarna notes that the Hebrew for 'work and keep' in Genesis 2:15 carries connotations of both productive labor and protective stewardship — human work is creative partnership with God, not mere occupation.

Longman reads Ecclesiastes' positive statements about work as the sage's affirmation that daily labor, enjoyed as a gift, is where most humans encounter meaning — grand purpose is less common than faithful presence.
MacArthur argues that Colossians 3:23 eliminates the sacred-secular divide in vocation — every believer's workplace becomes a venue for worship when work is offered consciously to the Lord.
